planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall, pressing 1–2 inches deep in dry, sandy, strongly acidic soil in full sun; dwarf live oak spreads primarily by underground rhizomes to form dense thickets in dry, sandy longleaf pine flatwoods and coastal scrub of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; dwarf live oak is a running, rhizomatous scrub oak of the longleaf pine flatwoods and coastal scrub habitats of the southeastern Coastal Plain. Unlike typical upright oaks, this species stays very low (usually under 3 feet) and spreads by underground rhizomes to form dense, thicket-like colonies — a fire-adapted growth form that allows it to re-sprout vigorously after ground fires in its native longleaf pine savanna habitat. The small, evergreen leaves are typical of live oaks. The acorns provide food for bobwhite quail, wild turkey, deer, and numerous other wildlife. An outstanding native for stabilizing dry, sandy soils and creating dense, low wildlife cover in the longleaf pine ecosystem. Its low habit makes it useful as a large-scale native ground cover in dry, sandy, sunny sites.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh acorns in fall in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; sandhill oak is endemic to the ancient scrub habitats of central Florida and is a legally protected Florida state plant. Never collect from the wild — obtain only nursery-grown plants.

directions: Plant in dry, sandy, strongly acidic, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; sandhill oak is a Florida scrub endemic — one of the most specialized and geographically restricted oaks in North America, found only in the ancient inland scrub habitats of central Florida's Lake Wales Ridge and surrounding areas. These scrub habitats on ancient sand dunes are among the most threatened ecosystems in the US, harboring numerous endemic species including the Florida scrub-jay, which depends on low scrub oaks like Q. inopina for acorn food and low, open habitat structure. The plant forms a spreading shrub to small tree with glossy, nearly evergreen leaves and is adapted to the driest, most nutrient-poor sandy soils of the ancient scrub ecosystem. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants from Florida-certified native nurseries; wild collection is illegal.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; stiff dogwood grows naturally in wet, moist bottomland woodlands, swamp edges, and stream banks of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; stiff dogwood is a native multi-stemmed shrub of wetland margins and moist bottomland forests throughout the southeastern Coastal Plain, distinguished from gray dogwood (C. racemosa) by its more southern range and adaptation to moist to wet conditions. The flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers in spring attract many native bees and flies. The small, blue-black berries on red stems in fall are critically important food for numerous bird species including warblers, thrushes, and vireos during fall migration — one of the most productive native bird-food shrubs for the Gulf South. Spreads by root sprouts to form multi-stemmed thickets in wet conditions. An essential native shrub for wet, shaded garden margins and naturalistic wetland plantings in zone 9a.

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy or rocky, infertile soil in full sun; pitch pine is adapted to the poorest, most fire-prone habitats of the eastern US — the Pine Barrens, rocky ridges, and sandy outwash plains where other trees cannot persist.

directions: Plant in dry, strongly acidic, sandy or rocky, infertile, well-drained soil in full sun; pitch pine is one of the most fire-adapted trees in eastern North America, able to re-sprout from dormant buds in the bark after fire damage — a trait unique among northeastern pines. The New Jersey Pine Barrens, one of the most ecologically important and unusual ecosystems in the eastern US, is dominated by pitch pine, which requires periodic fire to maintain the open, scrubby canopy and prevent succession to other forest types. The tree produces pitch (resin) in extraordinary quantities — historically harvested for naval stores, tar, and turpentine. An extremely tough, gnarled, characterful tree for the driest, most infertile, fire-maintained habitats in the Northeast and Appalachians. Not suitable for zone 9 — limited to zones 4–7.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall for spring germination after cold stratification; seeds require 3–4 months of cold stratification. Little silverbell grows in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade — a classic understory tree of the southeastern mixed forest.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; little silverbell is the smallest of the native silverbells (Halesia), forming a shrubby small tree to 25 feet with the genus's characteristic white, bell-shaped flowers in spring before leaf-out. The flowers hang in clusters along the bare winter stems and create a charming early spring display. The fruits develop the distinctive winged structure typical of the genus. This species is native to the Gulf Coastal Plain and Piedmont from South Carolina to Texas and is better adapted to the heat and humidity of the Gulf South (zone 9a) than the more commonly planted Carolina silverbell (H. tetraptera). An excellent native understory tree for partial shade in moist, acidic, woodland garden settings.
